attributes.stem: A 71-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with 3-hour history of progressively worsening right leg pain and numbness. His past medical history includes hypertension, for which he takes perindopril. On examination his blood pressure is 145/95 mmHg and an irregularly irregular pulse of 120bpm. Both femoral pulses are palpable however the right femoral pulse is considerably weaker than the left. He has normally palpable pedal pulses in his left leg. No pulses can be felt in his right leg below the groin. The right leg is pale and cool to touch. The patient does not have meaningful movement of his toes and has reduced sensation in the forefoot. He is diagnosed with a Rutherford class IIb Right leg acute limb ischaemia (ALI) and a heparin infusion is commenced as per local treatment protocol.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has ALI of the right leg in the presence of rapid atrial fibrillation, most likely due to a common femoral artery embolus which is usually secondary to a cardio-embolic aetiology, however further investigations will need to be completed. He requires rapid revascularisation to prevent further ischaemic damage to his limb. The management of acute limb ischaemia depends on several factors that includes the level and extent of vascular occlusion, severity of symptoms, aetiology and patient factors such as comorbidities, frailty and patient or family wishes. Treatment algorithms largely depend on surgical experience and available technology in a particular vascular unit. The European Society of Vascular Surgery (ESVS) guidelines recommend that ALI should be treated by Vascular specialists in a centre that is able to offer both open and endovascular therapy. The urgency of treatment is usually dictated by the Rutherford clinical classification, and it is especially imperative to clinically recognise the Acutely Threatened Leg (about 10% of cases of ALI) which requires re-vascularisation within 4-6 hours to avoid irreversible nerve and muscle damage. This is characterised by Pallor, Pulselessness, Perishing cold, Paraesthesia and Paralysis (5 P's).

This patient should undergo a diagnostic CT Angiography that will delineate the anatomy of the arterial pathology which in this case will most likely be due to an embolus in the common femoral artery. He will then need an urgent surgical embolectomy in the first instance.

A reminder that ALI is a sudden interruption of blood flow to the lower limb caused by

1)      Embolus commonly from cardiac/proximal aortic source (usually lodging at the common femoral artery bifurcation)

2)      Thrombosis of pre-existing atherosclerotic stenosed artery

3)      Thrombosis of arterial bypass graft, stent or peripheral aneurysm (usually a popliteal aneurysm)

CTA is the preferred initial investigation which then allows planning of the best form of intervention.

1)      Femoral embolus is best treated by femoral embolectomy, usually performed under local anaesthesia with anaesthetic support due to the cardiac risks on re-perfusion

2)      If the common femoral artery is thrombosed due to underlying disease this is best treated with surgical thrombectomy and endarterectomy of the atherosclerotic disease

3)      Distal femoral, popliteal and tibial thromboses are increasingly being treated with endovascular techniques (catheter directed thrombolysis, stenting etc) provided there is adequate radiological imaging and appropriate endovascular expertise. Otherwise, there is a role for surgical exploration, thrombectomy or bypass if necessary.

4)      Thrombosed popliteal aneurysms are a complex management issue with high risk of limb loss often due to the loss of distal tibial runoff. If runoff has been lost catheter directed thrombolysis followed by covered stent graft of the aneurysm is first line of treatment however if there is intact good runoff then localised popliteal bypass with reversed saphenous vein is also an excellent option if the patient is fit for surgery.
learningPoints: In an immediately threatened limb emergency embolectomy is the preferred treatment option.
references: Bjorck M, Earnshaw JJ, Acosta S et al. European Society for Vascular Surgery (ESVS) 2020 Clinical Practice Guidelines on the Management of Acute Limb Ischaemia. Our J Endovasc Surg 2020; 59:173-18.
Goldstein JA, Mishkel G. Choosing the correct therapeutic option for acute limb ischemia. Interv. Cardiol. (2011) 3(3), 381–89.
